proposals.title: Learning Implicit Belief States for Continuous Control: A Recurrent DDPG Approach to Partial Observability
proposals.motivation: Real-world embodied agents, from robotics to virtual characters, operate in partially observable environments where crucial state information is latent. Pre-2026, methods like DDPG excel in fully-observed continuous control but falter under partial observability, often requiring explicit state estimation modules. This work addresses the core challenge of learning effective policies directly from raw observation sequences in non-Markovian, continuous domains, a critical step toward more generalist agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing the feed-forward networks in DDPG with recurrent networks (LSTMs/GRUs) will enable the agent to learn an implicit, task-relevant belief state within its hidden activations, leading to more sample-efficient and robust policy learning in partially observable continuous control tasks compared to memoryless baselines.
proposals.method: We will develop Recurrent DDPG (RDDPG), where both the actor (policy) and critic (Q-function) are recurrent neural networks. The agent's hidden state is updated at each timestep, integrating the current partial observation and action. We will train the system end-to-end using a modified DDPG objective, with the recurrent networks backpropagated through time. Evaluation will be conducted on established partially observable MuJoCo benchmarks and novel 3D navigation tasks with occlusions, comparing against DDPG on observation history and explicit POMDP baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical, end-to-end trainable algorithm for continuous POMDPs that eliminates the need for hand-designed state estimators. It will provide empirical evidence on the sample efficiency and asymptotic performance of implicit belief learning in actor-critic frameworks and offer insights into the representations learned by recurrent agents in embodied settings.
proposals.novelty: While recurrent policies exist, their integration and rigorous evaluation within the off-policy, continuous-action DDPG framework for learning implicit belief states is not fully explored. The novelty lies in unifying the representational power of recurrent networks with the sample efficiency of deterministic off-policy learning for partial observability, moving beyond simple history concatenation.

proposals.title: Decoupled Successor Features for Stable On-Policy Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: On-policy deep RL methods like PPO are stable but sample-inefficient, while off-policy methods using large replay buffers are efficient but prone to instability and divergence, especially in non-stationary or partially observable environments. This trade-off is a fundamental bottleneck for training reliable, generalist embodied agents in complex virtual worlds. A method that achieves off-policy sample efficiency while retaining on-policy stability would be a significant advance.
proposals.hypothesis: Decoupling value estimation into a slow, policy-agnostic successor feature network and a fast, regularized on-policy actor will eliminate the divergence of off-policy learning while matching its sample efficiency, leading to more stable and data-efficient training.
proposals.method: We propose a two-component architecture: 1) A successor feature (SF) network, trained via TD learning on a rolling window of recent transitions to learn a generalized representation of environment dynamics and reward features, serving as a stable critic. 2) An on-policy actor network updated via a modified policy gradient (e.g., PPO-Clip), using the SF network for advantage estimation and constrained by a KL or state-distribution divergence penalty relative to policies within the data window. The SF network's slow, amortized updates provide a consistent learning target, breaking the destructive feedback loop between the policy and its value estimate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, stable deep RL algorithm that does not require a conventional replay buffer, theoretically grounded in successor representations. It will demonstrate improved training stability and sample efficiency on benchmarks for embodied AI and complex control, directly contributing to the development of more reliable generalist agents.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit decoupling and differential pacing of successor feature learning from policy optimization, combined with a distributional constraint based on recent policy data. This integrates the benefits of successor representations for transfer and off-policy efficiency with the convergence guarantees of on-policy methods, a synthesis not explored in prior SF or stable RL work.

proposals.title: ClonalDDPG: On-Policy Deep Reinforcement Learning via Population-Based Clonal Selection
proposals.motivation: Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) and its successors rely on off-policy replay buffers for stability and sample efficiency, but these buffers introduce distributional shift, require corrective importance sampling, and can become computationally burdensome. An on-policy alternative that matches the efficiency of buffer-based methods would simplify algorithm design and theory while improving stability in non-stationary environments, a key challenge for embodied agents in complex virtual worlds.
proposals.hypothesis: A population-based actor-critic algorithm, inspired by the clonal selection principle of adaptive immune systems, can achieve stable and sample-efficient learning in continuous control tasks without an off-policy experience replay buffer, by performing selective amplification and mutation of policies based on their performance on freshly generated on-policy data.
proposals.method: We will develop ClonalDDPG, which maintains a population of actor-critic network pairs. Each iteration, we collect a batch of experience using a policy derived from the current population (e.g., the mean or a selected elite). We then evaluate each population member's critic on this new batch to assess fitness. High-fitness 'clones' are amplified via weighted averaging into the new policy parameters, while targeted parameter noise (inspired by directed exploration or evolutionary strategies) is applied to generate diversity. This process replaces the replay buffer's uniform sampling with a selective, on-policy update, aligning policy evaluation and improvement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, biologically-inspired paradigm for on-policy deep RL in continuous action spaces, potentially eliminating the need for off-policy corrections. It will contribute a new algorithm class that unifies population-based search with policy gradient methods, offering insights into the unification of learning and adaptation mechanisms, a core theme in generalist AI research.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is directly replacing the DDPG replay buffer with an on-policy, population-based clonal selection mechanism, a radical departure from standard off-policy actor-critic dogma. It draws a non-obvious analogy from immunology to address a fundamental RL stability problem, merging evolutionary and gradient-based optimization in a novel way.

proposals.title: Gradient Surgery and Modular Gating for Interference-Robust Multi-Physics Agents
proposals.motivation: Generalist embodied agents, like SIMA, must master diverse tasks across virtual worlds with varying physics. However, training a single policy on multiple physics domains often leads to catastrophic interference, where learning a new task degrades performance on previously learned ones. This instability prevents robust, scalable multi-task learning, a core challenge for agents operating in open-ended environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly managing gradient conflicts via projection techniques, combined with a learned modular architecture, will enable a single policy to achieve stable, positive transfer across disparate physics tasks while mitigating destructive interference.
proposals.method: We will build a policy network with a shared feature backbone and task-conditional output heads. During multi-task training, we will apply PCGrad or a similar gradient surgery algorithm to project conflicting gradients for shared parameters into compatible directions before updates. Concurrently, we will train a lightweight, relational gating network—inspired by modular and mixture-of-experts principles—to dynamically route activations through specialized, sparsely activated sub-networks based on inferred task or physics context.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a concrete method for training interference-robust, multi-physics agents, advancing the field of generalist embodied AI. It will provide new empirical insights into the relationship between gradient conflict, modularity, and transfer in reinforcement learning, bridging concepts from supervised multi-task learning and embodied reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While gradient surgery has been studied in supervised learning, its application to stabilize multi-physics RL policies is novel. Combining it with a learned, context-aware gating mechanism for modular adaptation within a single agent architecture represents a significant integration of distinct pre-2026 techniques for a new purpose.

proposals.title: SkillNet: Modular Policy Composition for Cross-Domain Physics Generalization
proposals.motivation: Current deep reinforcement learning agents, like those trained with DDPG, are typically specialized to a single task or physics environment, requiring expensive retraining for new domains. This brittleness limits the development of generalist embodied agents capable of operating in diverse virtual worlds, a core challenge in pre-2026 AI research. Drawing inspiration from biological modularity, we propose that learning reusable skill primitives is key to efficient and adaptable physical reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: An agent that learns a shared library of primitive skill modules via off-policy RL, combined with a task-conditioned meta-controller for dynamic composition, will demonstrate superior sample efficiency and zero-shot generalization across multiple, distinct physics-based tasks compared to monolithic or finetuned policies.
proposals.method: We will extend the DDPG framework to learn a library of skill modules, implemented as small neural networks, each parameterizing a reusable policy primitive (e.g., apply force, maintain balance). A relational hypernetwork, inspired by graph-based reasoning, will act as the meta-controller, taking the task description (as a graph or scene representation) and dynamically generating the weights to compose relevant skill modules into a coherent policy. Training will use a multi-task objective across a suite of MuJoCo and custom physics tasks, encouraging module reuse and sparsity.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architecture for modular policy learning that explicitly separates skill acquisition from compositional reasoning. It will provide empirical evidence for the benefits of modularity in RL for cross-domain generalization, moving toward more adaptable embodied agents. The released library of interpretable skill modules could serve as a foundation for future research in skill-based RL.
proposals.novelty: While modular RL and skill discovery exist, our approach uniquely draws a direct analogy to Evo-Devo's genetic toolkit, framing skill modules as conserved, reusable components and the composer as a developmental program. The use of a relational hypernetwork for dynamic, task-specific composition of low-level policy networks is a novel technical departure from prior skill-chaining or option-based methods.

proposals.title: A Relational State-Space Policy for Multi-Physics Generalization in Deep Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) has excelled at single, well-defined tasks, but this narrow focus is a poor model for real-world learning where agents must adapt to diverse physical dynamics. The assumption that a policy is trained for a single physics model limits sample efficiency and robustness. This work addresses the challenge of learning a single, generalist policy that can operate across multiple, distinct physics environments, a key step toward more capable and flexible embodied agents.
proposals.hypothesis: A DDPG-based agent equipped with a relational neural network that explicitly models object interactions and a task-conditioned state representation can learn a unified policy that generalizes zero-shot to unseen physics-based tasks, outperforming single-task specialists and naive multi-task baselines.
proposals.method: We will modify the DDPG architecture by integrating a relational module, inspired by graph networks, into the critic and actor to process object-centric state representations. The state will be augmented with a learnable physics context vector, inferred from recent transitions, to condition the policy. Training will occur across a curated suite of MuJoCo tasks with varied dynamics (e.g., different gravity, friction, body masses). We will benchmark against single-task DDPG, a multi-task hard-parameter sharing baseline, and analyze generalization to held-out physics configurations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete architecture and methodology for learning physics-general policies in continuous control, moving beyond the single-task paradigm. It will contribute new insights into how relational inductive biases and context conditioning can enable cross-domain generalization in RL, a core challenge for generalist embodied agents like SIMA.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task RL and domain randomization exist, explicitly structuring the policy to reason about relational physics and infer a disentangled dynamics context for zero-shot generalization across fundamentally different physical tasks is a novel combination, bridging Wierstra's interests in graphs, state, and generalist learning.

proposals.title: Residual Dynamics Adaptation: Bridging the Sim-to-Real Gap with Online Graph-Structured Policy Refinement
proposals.motivation: The simulation-to-reality (sim-to-real) gap remains a critical bottleneck for deploying learned robotic policies, as unmodeled dynamics and perceptual discrepancies cause failures. Pre-2026, domain adaptation often relies on extensive randomization or offline fine-tuning, which is either inefficient or unsafe for online policy execution on physical hardware. A method for safe, sample-efficient online adaptation during a robot's initial real-world deployment is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A DDPG-based policy, pre-trained in simulation, can achieve safe and efficient real-world transfer by augmenting its actor-critic network with a lightweight, graph-structured module that learns to predict and compensate for a residual in the dynamics online, conditioned on the current state and action.
proposals.method: We will extend the DDPG architecture with a relational network that takes the current state and action as a graph and outputs a dynamics residual. This residual predictor is trained online via a model-based loss against observed state transitions, while its updates are constrained by a trust region to ensure policy stability. The core policy is initially frozen; the adaptation module learns during a safeguarded initial real-world phase, after which the full policy can be fine-tuned.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architecture for online sim-to-real transfer that explicitly decouples known dynamics from learned residuals, improving safety during adaptation. It will provide empirical validation on a real-world robotic manipulator, demonstrating superior sample efficiency and final task performance compared to direct transfer and standard fine-tuning baselines.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines graph-structured relational reasoning for residual modeling with constrained online learning within an off-policy RL framework, moving beyond domain randomization or system identification to enable continuous, in-situ policy correction.

proposals.title: Planning-Augmented Policy Gradients: Integrating Model-Based Rollouts with DDPG for Sample-Efficient Embodied Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Model-free deep RL algorithms like DDPG are sample-inefficient and struggle with long-horizon reasoning, limiting their application in complex embodied tasks. Conversely, pure model-based planning often fails due to compounding model errors. A principled integration could leverage the strengths of both, a critical step toward generalist agents capable of learning and reasoning in rich virtual worlds, aligning with the SIMA research direction.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting the DDPG actor-critic framework with short-horizon rollouts from a learned latent dynamics model will significantly improve sample efficiency and long-term credit assignment without sacrificing the asymptotic performance of model-free learning.
proposals.method: We propose a hybrid architecture where a relational dynamics model, trained on the agent's experience, operates in a latent state space informed by the critic. During DDPG policy updates, we generate imagined rollouts from this model. The actor is trained on a combined objective: the standard DDPG policy gradient plus a term maximizing the predicted Q-values over these short imagined trajectories. This injects model-based planning directly into the policy optimization, encouraging foresight.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, scalable framework for combining model-free and model-based RL, directly improving sample efficiency for embodied agents. It will offer new insights into how learned internal models can guide policy learning, contributing to the unification of learning and reasoning paradigms for agents in complex graph-like environments.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior hybrid approaches that use models only for data augmentation or separate planning loops, our method tightly integrates imagined rollouts into the DDPG gradient update itself. This leverages the model specifically to address over-squashing in the credit assignment graph of the policy, a novel perspective informed by recent graph-based analyses of RL.

proposals.title: Stabilizing Relational Scene Understanding in Embodied Agents with Natural Policy Gradients
proposals.motivation: In pre-2026 research, embodied agents like SIMA must learn complex, long-horizon tasks in rich virtual worlds, requiring stable policy learning. Standard policy gradient methods can suffer from high variance and catastrophic updates, which is especially detrimental when the agent's state representation involves relational reasoning over scene graphs or entities. This instability hinders the reliable acquisition of robust, generalist behaviors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying natural policy gradient methods, specifically Trust Region Policy Optimization (TRPO) or its approximations, to the actor update in a graph-based actor-critic architecture will significantly stabilize training and improve sample efficiency for embodied agents performing tasks requiring relational scene understanding.
proposals.method: We will integrate TRPO into the actor-learner framework of an agent like SIMA 2. The agent's policy network will process a relational scene graph (addressing over-squashing via methods from 'Leave Graphs Alone'). The critic will provide a value estimate. The core innovation is to constrain each actor parameter update using the natural gradient, computed via the Fisher Information Matrix derived from the policy distribution over actions conditioned on the graph state. This will enforce a trust region, preventing overly large, performance-collapsing steps.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a principled method for stabilizing policy learning in graph-structured, embodied AI. It will demonstrate improved training stability and final performance on complex, multi-stage tasks in 3D simulated environments. The findings will bridge advanced policy optimization theory with practical architectures for relational reasoning and embodiment.
proposals.novelty: While TRPO is known in standard RL, its application to stabilize actors that operate on complex relational graph states, as used in cutting-edge embodied agents, is novel. This directly addresses the unique instability challenges arising from coupling graph neural networks with policy gradients in long-horizon, partially observable settings.

proposals.title: Curriculum Learning for Relational Reasoning in Embodied Agents
proposals.motivation: Generalist embodied agents like SIMA 2 must master complex, long-horizon tasks in rich 3D worlds, which presents a severe exploration and credit assignment challenge for policy search. Pre-2026, curriculum learning is a powerful but often ad-hoc technique for structuring experience. A principled, automated curriculum that leverages the relational structure of tasks and environments could dramatically improve sample efficiency and final performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum automatically generated by progressively increasing the complexity of the relational graph representing an agent's environment and goals will lead to more efficient learning and superior generalization in embodied agents compared to flat training or hand-designed curricula.
proposals.method: We will extend the SIMA 2 architecture with a curriculum generator that operates on a learned or provided scene graph. Starting with simplified sub-graphs (e.g., fewer objects, simpler spatial relations), the agent will train on progressively more complex relational states. We will adapt methods from 'Leave Graphs Alone' to ensure gradient flow through the evolving complexity, and use the agent's learning progress (e.g., success rate derivative) to automatically schedule transitions between curriculum stages, moving 'toward' the full, complex task.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework for curriculum generation in relational, embodied domains, directly applicable to next-generation generalist agents. It will yield empirical results on complex 3D benchmarks demonstrating significant gains in sample efficiency and asymptotic performance. The project will also contribute insights into the interaction between curriculum design, graph-structured representations, and learning dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning is well-studied, its automated application to the *relational complexity* of a scene graph for embodied AI is novel. This approach moves beyond simple task sequences to structurally modify the agent's perceptual and reasoning environment, directly addressing the core challenge of learning in compositional worlds.

proposals.title: Inverse Dynamics Learning via Implicit Policy-Environment Duality in Actor-Critic Architectures
proposals.motivation: Inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) focuses on inferring reward functions from expert behavior, but the underlying environment dynamics are often assumed known. For robust imitation and transfer in complex, relational environments (e.g., embodied agents in virtual worlds like SIMA), learning the dynamics model is equally critical. This proposal addresses the inverse problem of inferring latent transition dynamics given only an optimal policy, a key step toward agents that can reason about and generalize across unseen environments.
proposals.hypothesis: The structure of an optimal policy, as represented by a trained actor-critic network, implicitly encodes sufficient constraints to recover a consistent, parsimonious model of the environment's transition dynamics, even in partially observable or graph-structured state spaces.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as a constrained optimization: given a fixed, pre-trained actor-critic pair (policy π* and value function V*), we train a dynamics model (a neural network or graph network) whose predictions, when used with the known reward function in the Bellman optimality equation, make π* and V* appear optimal. We will leverage relational networks to handle graph-based state representations (addressing over-squashing via architecture, not rewiring) and employ regularization inspired by double-descent phenomena to ensure generalization. The training objective minimizes the Bellman error of the given value function under the learned dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for sample-efficient environment model acquisition from expert policies, advancing model-based imitation learning. It will yield theoretical insights into the information-theoretic relationship between optimal policies and dynamics, and offer a practical tool for bootstrapping world models in generalist embodied agents without exhaustive interaction.
proposals.novelty: While IRL infers rewards, and forward RL learns policies from dynamics, directly inferring dynamics from an optimal policy using the fixed-point structure of actor-critic learning is an underexplored inverse problem. The approach uniquely combines insights from the duality of policy and value functions with modern relational architectures for scalable inference.

proposals.title: Scene-Graph Policy Networks: Learning Embodied Action from a Single Frame
proposals.motivation: Current embodied AI agents, like SIMA, rely heavily on processing sequences of frames to infer state, dynamics, and affordances, which is computationally expensive and data-hungry. This work challenges the assumption that temporal history is strictly necessary for effective policy learning in many environments. By focusing on learning from single static images, we aim to create more efficient, interpretable, and generalizable agents that can reason about action possibilities directly from scene structure.
proposals.hypothesis: A policy can be effectively learned from a single static image by explicitly modeling the relational structure of the scene and the agent's internal state, obviating the need for frame sequences in environments where the core challenge is spatial and relational reasoning rather than precise dynamics prediction.
proposals.method: We will develop a Scene-Graph Policy Network (SGPN). A vision transformer will encode a single RGB frame into a set of object-centric embeddings. These will be structured into a scene graph using a relational network, explicitly capturing object properties and spatial relationships. This graph, concatenated with a learned or recurrently maintained agent state vector (e.g., goal, inventory), will be processed by a Graph Neural Network. The GNN's output will feed into a policy head predicting actions. We will benchmark this against sequence-based baselines (e.g., on SIMA-like tasks in Minecraft) and analyze failure modes to understand the limits of static-frame reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, radically simplified architecture for embodied agents in static or semi-static environments, significantly reducing inference compute. It will yield new insights into what constitutes a sufficient 'state' for decision-making, potentially unifying ideas from scene understanding, relational reasoning, and efficient policy learning. It also offers a clearer path toward interpretability through the explicit scene graph representation.
proposals.novelty: The proposal directly challenges the foundational use of frame sequences in deep RL for embodied AI, proposing to replace temporal convolution with explicit spatial-relational reasoning. It uniquely combines Wierstra's interests in scene graphs, relational models, and agent state, applying them to the core policy learning problem rather than auxiliary tasks.

proposals.title: Stateful Networks for Online Reinforcement Learning: Eliminating Replay via Implicit Experience Encoding
proposals.motivation: Replay buffers are a cornerstone of modern deep reinforcement learning (RL), enabling stable learning from off-policy data. However, they introduce significant memory overhead, computational complexity for sampling/prioritization, and can become a bottleneck for truly online, lifelong learning in embodied agents like SIMA. Pre-2026, methods like neural episodic control or model-based RL offer alternatives but often compromise on sample efficiency or generality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a recurrent agent network, with its internal state structured and updated via relational reasoning mechanisms, can implicitly encode a sufficient statistic of past experience to enable stable, sample-efficient policy learning without a separate replay store.
proposals.method: We will develop a recurrent agent architecture where the internal state vector functions as a dynamic, structured memory. Inspired by relational networks and graph neural networks (addressing over-squashing), we will design a state-update mechanism that treats the state as a graph of latent entities and relations, updated via attention over the current transition. Learning will be driven by a combination of policy gradients (e.g., V-trace for off-policy correction) and auxiliary self-supervised objectives that encourage the state to retain predictive information about recent returns and observations, mimicking the consistency targets used in unification studies of grokking and double descent.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new class of online RL algorithms that are more memory-efficient and conceptually aligned with continual learning. It will provide a practical advance for training embodied agents in persistent virtual worlds and offer theoretical insights into how neural networks can internally subsume the function of an experience replay buffer.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly designing the agent's internal state dynamics—using relational inductive biases—to obviate the need for replay, moving beyond simply using recurrence within a standard replay-based algorithm like R2D2. This integrates themes of state, relational reasoning, and learning dynamics in a novel way for RL.

proposals.title: Policy-Centric World Models: Learning Scene-Aware Action Policies for Embodied Agents without Value Functions
proposals.motivation: Modern embodied agents, like SIMA, must learn from high-dimensional sensory inputs (e.g., pixels) to act in complex, relational 3D worlds. While Q-learning and other value-based methods have been dominant, they can be sample-inefficient and struggle with complex credit assignment in long-horizon tasks. Direct policy search, particularly when combined with world models, offers a promising alternative for learning stable, scene-aware behaviors directly from sensory streams.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model-based, policy-centric architecture, which directly learns a scene-graph-conditioned action policy via gradient ascent, will outperform value-based counterparts in sample efficiency and final performance on long-horizon, relational reasoning tasks in virtual environments.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel agent architecture where a perception module builds a dynamic scene graph from sensory input. This graph will condition a learned world model (a stochastic state-space model) which predicts future graph states. A deterministic policy network, parameterized as a Graph Neural Network (GNN), will map directly from the current and predicted graph states to actions. The policy will be trained end-to-end via backpropagation through the world model using policy gradient methods (e.g., REINFORCE with baseline or PPO), explicitly avoiding Q-value estimation. We will benchmark against SIMA 2 and other baselines in 3D simulation platforms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical comparison of policy-centric vs. value-centric paradigms for embodied AI in rich sensory domains. It will contribute a new, scalable architecture for learning directly from pixels to actions via intermediate structured representations, advancing the integration of learning, reasoning, and scene understanding.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate exclusion of value functions within a model-based agent, and the explicit fusion of dynamic scene graphs (addressing over-squashing) with policy gradient optimization for embodied control, a synthesis not fully explored in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Graph-Based Skill Discovery for Generalist Embodied Agents
proposals.motivation: Generalist embodied agents like SIMA 2 must master long-horizon tasks in complex, relational environments, but flat reinforcement learning struggles with sample efficiency and credit assignment. Hierarchical RL (HRL) offers a promising path by decomposing tasks, yet pre-2026 methods often rely on pre-defined skills or struggle to discover reusable, temporally extended abstractions in open-ended worlds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical agent, which discovers graph-structured skills over latent environment states and learns to reason over their relational dependencies, will achieve superior sample efficiency and generalization in long-horizon embodied tasks compared to monolithic or flat skill-based approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-level HRL architecture. A high-level graph neural network (GNN) policy will operate on a dynamic latent state graph, representing objects and their relations, to select from a learned library of low-level skills. Skills will be discovered end-to-end using an information-theoretic objective that encourages diversity and temporal abstraction, building on variational intrinsic control principles. The low-level skills will be trained with off-policy RL (e.g., SAC), while the high-level GNN policy will be trained with a relational variant of PPO, leveraging the agent's own experience and the SIMA 2 framework for environment interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified framework for joint skill discovery and hierarchical graph-based reasoning in embodied AI. It will provide empirical evidence on how relational abstractions improve HRL sample efficiency and offer new insights into the synergy between graph-structured representations and temporal abstraction for complex task decomposition.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of end-to-end graph-relational reasoning (inspired by 'Leave Graphs Alone') with unsupervised skill discovery within a generalist agent paradigm, moving beyond pre-defined skill sets or non-relational HRL formulations common in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Curriculum Learning for Structured World Models: Efficient Skill Acquisition in Relational Environments
proposals.motivation: Training generalist embodied agents like SIMA 2 requires efficient learning of complex, long-horizon tasks in structured virtual worlds. Current RL methods often struggle with sample inefficiency and exploration in such relational, graph-like state spaces. While curriculum learning is a powerful principle in supervised learning, its systematic application to RL, particularly for agents that explicitly reason about object and scene structure, remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum over the relational complexity of the environment—progressively increasing the number of objects, their interactions, or the required relational reasoning—will lead to more sample-efficient learning and better final performance for agents using graph-based or relational world models.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that automatically generates a curriculum of tasks within a simulator (e.g., Unity-based). The curriculum will be defined by a difficulty metric operating on the underlying environment graph, such as the number of nodes (objects), edge density (relations), or required planning depth. We will train a relational agent architecture, building on SIMA's foundation, using a state-of-the-art RL algorithm (e.g., PPO). The agent's learning progress will be monitored to guide progression through the curriculum, dynamically adjusting task difficulty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for structuring training environments to accelerate the learning of relational and compositional skills. It will yield new insights into how curriculum design interacts with graph-structured state representations, potentially addressing challenges like over-squashing by gradual exposure. The resulting framework will be a step toward more data-efficient training of generalist embodied agents.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly linking curriculum design to the formal relational structure (graph properties) of the environment, moving beyond simple scalar metrics like reward or state space size. This directly connects curriculum learning to the agent's internal graph-based reasoning processes, a synergy not deeply explored in pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Relational State Networks for Cross-Domain Transfer in Atari: Unifying Scene Understanding and Policy Generalization
proposals.motivation: While deep RL agents can master individual Atari games, they require immense, task-specific data and fail to leverage common structures across games, such as object interactions or spatial-temporal dynamics. This inefficiency is a major barrier to developing generalist embodied agents, as highlighted by projects like SIMA. Transfer learning across games remains a significant open challenge, with prior work often limited by rigid feature representations that do not capture the underlying relational scene semantics shared between different game environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a relational state-space model, trained to extract and reason over object-centric graph representations of game scenes, will enable effective transfer of learned features and policies between distinct Atari games, significantly accelerating learning in new domains compared to training from scratch.
proposals.method: We will extend the relational architectures explored in 'Leave Graphs Alone' and the state-space models from Wierstra's prior work. Our agent will use an encoder to decompose an observed game frame into a set of object embeddings and their relational graph. This graph-structured state will be processed by a relational network to predict actions. For transfer, we will pre-train the encoder and relational reasoning module on a source game (e.g., Montezuma's Revenge for its complex scenes) and then fine-tune only the final policy head on a target game (e.g., Pitfall!). We will benchmark against standard CNN-based agents and ablation models without the relational component, measuring sample efficiency and final performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework for knowledge transfer in RL based on structured, relational scene understanding, moving beyond pixel-level similarity. It will contribute new insights into how object-centric representations can bridge the 'sim-to-real' gap within virtual worlds, directly advancing the goal of generalist agents as in SIMA. The findings will also inform the unification of learning phenomena like grokking and double descent in the context of multi-task transfer.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly combining object-relational graph representations—designed to mitigate over-squashing—with transfer learning for RL, rather than applying standard network fine-tuning. This approach directly tests whether abstracting game 'scenes' into relational structures is the key compositional prior needed for generalization across environments, a non-obvious step beyond prior feature-sharing methods.

proposals.title: Gradient-Informed Pathway Evolution for Relational Reasoning in Embodied Agents
proposals.motivation: Evolutionary strategies (ES) and gradient-based learning are powerful but largely separate paradigms for optimizing neural networks. ES excels at exploring structural and architectural changes, while gradients provide efficient local search. In complex, relational reasoning tasks for embodied agents—like those in SIMA—the pathways for information flow (e.g., in graph neural networks or scene graphs) are critical but often fixed or evolved blindly. Combining these paradigms could unlock more efficient search for optimal computational pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: Using gradient information to directly bias the mutation operators in an evolutionary algorithm will lead to faster and more effective discovery of high-performing neural pathways for relational reasoning, compared to standard ES or pure gradient-based architectural search.
proposals.method: We propose a novel algorithm, Gradient-Informed Pathway Evolution (GIPE), building on Daan Wierstra's expertise in ES and relational networks. GIPE maintains a population of computational pathway graphs (e.g., message-passing structures over a scene graph). The key innovation is that the mutation operator for adding, removing, or rewiring edges is not random but is guided by the gradient of a meta-loss (e.g., task loss sensitivity) with respect to a continuous relaxation of the graph's adjacency matrix. This couples evolutionary exploration with gradient-based local saliency, implemented within a training framework akin to PPO for policy networks in embodied environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new hybrid optimization method for neural architecture search, specifically tailored for graph-based reasoning modules. It will demonstrate improved sample efficiency and final performance on relational tasks in simulated 3D environments (e.g., extended SIMA benchmarks). The results will offer insights into unifying evolutionary and gradient-based learning principles.
proposals.novelty: While gradient-based NAS (e.g., DARTS) and ES exist, directly using gradients to mutate discrete graph structures in an evolutionary population—rather than to update parameters or architecture weights—is a novel synthesis. It addresses the over-squashing problem by actively searching for better pathways without the rewiring constraints of prior graph-based methods.

proposals.title: Scaling PathNet: Investigating Emergent Modularity and Catastrophic Forgetting in Massively Super-Networks
proposals.motivation: PathNet demonstrated a promising path towards continual learning and multi-task intelligence via a sparse, modular super-network. However, its original scale was limited. Scaling such architectures is crucial for testing their viability as a substrate for generalist embodied agents like SIMA, which must learn and reason across vast, sequential task graphs in complex virtual worlds. This directly addresses core challenges in learning and reasoning for scene understanding and human-AI interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling PathNet to significantly larger super-networks and longer, more diverse task sequences will lead to emergent, structured modularity that mitigates catastrophic forgetting more effectively than smaller networks, while revealing new scaling laws related to grokking and double descent phenomena in sparse, modular architectures.
proposals.method: We will scale the original PathNet framework by constructing super-networks with orders of magnitude more modules and pathways, training them on extensive, structured sequences of tasks derived from domains like Atari, 3D navigation (e.g., Procgen, DMLab), and relational reasoning puzzles. We will employ advanced evolutionary strategies for pathway selection and integrate relational network components within modules to enhance reasoning. Performance will be analyzed through metrics of forward/backward transfer, forgetting, and the emergence of reusable functional subgraphs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale empirical analysis of PathNet's scaling properties, establishing benchmarks for modular continual learning. It will contribute new insights into the relationship between network sparsity, modularity, and multi-task scaling laws, directly informing the development of more capable and efficient generalist agents for virtual environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored scaling dense networks or static sparsity, systematically scaling a dynamic, sparse, modular super-network for continual learning across a large graph of complex tasks is unexplored. This bridges Wierstra's themes of learning, graphs, and state-based agents in a novel, scaled experimental regime.

proposals.title: Evolving Structured Policies: A Neuro-Graph Evolutionary Algorithm for Long-Horizon Embodied Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Evolutionary strategies are powerful for optimizing neural network parameters in complex, non-differentiable environments, as seen in pre-2026 work like OpenAI ES. However, scaling them to discover structured, long-horizon policies for embodied agents, such as those in the SIMA framework, remains inefficient. The challenge is to evolve not just weights but also the relational reasoning and state-space exploration strategies crucial for tasks requiring scene understanding and multi-step planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that co-evolving a population of agents with explicit graph-structured relational modules and a shared, learned state-space prior will significantly accelerate the discovery of high-performing, generalizable policies for long-horizon tasks compared to standard genetic algorithms or policy gradient methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a Neuro-Graph Evolutionary Algorithm (NGEA). Each agent comprises a core network (e.g., Transformer) and a dynamic relational graph module for scene reasoning. Evolution operates on two levels: (1) a population of agent parameters via a scalable evolutionary algorithm (e.g., CMA-ES), and (2) the connectivity and message-passing functions within each agent's graph module, informed by pre-2026 insights on avoiding over-squashing. A key innovation is a shared, learnable 'skill prior' network, trained via contrastive learning on population elites, which guides mutation toward promising regions of the state and skill space.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, sample-efficient evolutionary framework for training complex embodied agents. It will provide new insights into combining population-based search with structured neural architectures, specifically for relational reasoning tasks. The resulting method aims to set a new benchmark for evolving agents capable of sophisticated, long-horizon interaction in simulated 3D worlds.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit co-evolution of neural parameters and graph-relational structures within an embodied agent, guided by a learned state-space prior. This moves beyond standard weight-only evolution and static architectures, directly addressing the compositionality and long-term credit assignment problems in a unified search paradigm.

proposals.title: Bandit-Based Pathway Selection for Efficient Compositional Generalization in Embodied Agents
proposals.motivation: Large-scale neural agents, such as SIMA, learn diverse skills but face combinatorial explosion when composing them for novel tasks. Current methods like tournament selection for pathway exploration are heuristic and sample-inefficient, struggling to balance exploration of new skill combinations with exploitation of known effective ones. This limits an agent's ability to rapidly adapt and reason in complex, open-ended virtual worlds.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing heuristic selection with structured multi-armed bandit algorithms will enable more sample-efficient online exploration of compositional action pathways, leading to faster adaptation and superior zero-shot generalization in long-horizon embodied reasoning tasks.
proposals.method: We will integrate contextual bandits (e.g., LinUCB) and non-stationary bandits (e.g., EXP3) into the SIMA agent architecture. Each 'arm' corresponds to a latent skill or sub-policy pathway. The bandit's context will be derived from the agent's current state representation (scene/graph) and task goal. We will benchmark against tournament selection and random baselines on procedurally generated 3D environments requiring novel object manipulation and navigation sequences, measuring sample efficiency and success rate on held-out task distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, online decision-making framework for compositional exploration in embodied AI, moving beyond ad-hoc selection. It will yield concrete evidence on how bandit algorithms can mitigate the exploration bottleneck in large action spaces, contributing to both the machine learning (bandits for RL) and embodied agent communities.
proposals.novelty: While bandits are well-studied for discrete action selection, their application for adaptive selection of *latent compositional pathways* in a pre-trained, generalist embodied agent is novel. This bridges the gap between online decision theory and modular neural policy networks without altering the underlying graph structure of the policy ensemble.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning a Graph-Structured Policy for Compositional Task Pathway Discovery
proposals.motivation: Generalist embodied agents, such as SIMA, face a fundamental challenge in efficiently discovering viable action sequences (pathways) for novel tasks in complex virtual worlds. Current methods often rely on brute-force search or task-specific fine-tuning, which is sample-inefficient and lacks the compositional reasoning needed for rapid adaptation. This work addresses the core problem of learning a general policy for pathway search that can leverage past experience to structure exploration in new environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-controller, trained on a distribution of tasks and their solution pathways, can learn a graph-structured search policy that generalizes to novel tasks by efficiently proposing and refining candidate action sequences, significantly outperforming non-meta-learned baselines and one-shot planning methods.
proposals.method: We propose a meta-reinforcement learning framework where the outer loop trains a controller using MAML or Reptile. The controller's internal state is a relational graph network, encoding the current world state, task specification, and a dynamically built graph of explored partial pathways. This architecture, inspired by the 'Leave Graphs Alone' principle, processes this graph to propose the next promising action or sub-goal, forming a search policy. The inner-loop adaptation involves rolling out this policy on a new task for a few episodes to rapidly specialize.
proposals.contribution: This research will yield a novel meta-learning algorithm for efficient compositional search in embodied AI. It will provide a principled framework unifying meta-learning with relational reasoning over search graphs, advancing the sample efficiency and generalization of agents like SIMA. The work will also offer insights into the learned inductive biases for pathway discovery.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning and graph networks are established, their integration to learn a *search policy over a dynamically constructed pathway graph* is novel. This moves beyond using graphs for fixed relational reasoning and instead uses them to meta-learn how to explore and compose action sequences, directly addressing the 'how to search' problem in new task spaces.

proposals.title: Massively Parallel Evolution of Modular Neural Pathways via Asynchronous Population-Based Training
proposals.motivation: PathNet and similar modular neural architectures offer a promising route toward scalable, compositional learning and reasoning by dynamically routing information through specialized sub-networks. However, their evolution via population-based training is computationally prohibitive, limiting exploration of complex, long-horizon tasks in rich virtual environments. A scalable, distributed evolution framework is needed to unlock their potential for generalist embodied agents.
proposals.hypothesis: A massively parallel, asynchronous evolutionary system can efficiently discover high-performing, generalizable pathway structures for modular networks, overcoming the computational bottlenecks of sequential evolution and leading to agents with superior sample efficiency and task compositionality.
proposals.method: We will develop a distributed system where each worker machine runs an independent instance of a virtual environment (inspired by SIMA 2's 3D worlds) and asynchronously evaluates a candidate Pathway Module. A central coordinator maintains a population of modules, implemented as relational graphs over neural primitives, and performs asynchronous genetic operations (mutation, crossover) based on fitness signals (e.g., task reward, skill diversity). The state of each agent will be a composition of active pathways, evolved without explicit graph rewiring to avoid over-squashing, aligning with insights from 'Leave Graphs Alone'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, open-source framework for evolving large-scale modular neural networks, directly applicable to training embodied agents. It will yield empirical insights into the relationship between evolutionary scale, pathway complexity, and emergent generalization, contributing to the unification of large-scale optimization and architectural search.
proposals.novelty: While distributed training is common, applying massive, asynchronous population-based methods specifically to the evolution of dynamic internal pathway graphs—rather than just weights—is novel. This approach explicitly decouples architectural evolution from weight optimization, enabling exploration at a structural scale previously infeasible.

proposals.title: COG-BENCH: A Systematic Framework for Evaluating Combinatorial Generalization in Learning Systems
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in deep learning, models often fail to generalize systematically to novel combinations of known primitives, a core aspect of human reasoning. This limitation is critical for applications in embodied agents (like SIMA) and relational reasoning, where agents must operate in unseen but structurally familiar environments. Current benchmarks are ad-hoc, making it difficult to diagnose failure modes or measure genuine progress.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic benchmark, which explicitly disentangles and controls for compositionality, will reveal that current state-of-the-art models (including graph networks and relational architectures) exhibit predictable, architecture-dependent failure patterns on combinatorial tasks, and that performance will correlate with their ability to manage long-range dependencies without over-squashing.
proposals.method: We will design a generative framework to create benchmark families across domains (graph reasoning, 3D scene understanding, and text-based logic). Each family will systematically vary the complexity of compositions (e.g., object relations, spatial predicates, or logical clauses) while holding primitives constant. We will evaluate a suite of models, including Transformers, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), and relational architectures, analyzing performance against theoretical measures of compositionality and graph connectivity. The benchmark will integrate with existing frameworks like JAX and produce standardized diagnostic reports.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified, rigorous diagnostic tool for combinatorial generalization, enabling precise comparisons across architectures. It will produce actionable insights into the links between structural biases (e.g., in GNNs), training dynamics (e.g., grokking), and systematic generalization, guiding future model design for reasoning and embodied AI.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing task-specific benchmarks, COG-BENCH provides a controlled, generative framework that systematically isolates the combinatorial generalization axis. It directly connects benchmark design to recent theoretical insights on over-squashing in graphs and double descent phenomena, creating a direct testbed for these theories.

proposals.title: Generative Graph Networks for Structured Data Synthesis in Embodied Learning
proposals.motivation: Training robust embodied agents like SIMA requires vast, diverse datasets of structured interactions in virtual worlds, which are expensive and time-consuming to produce. While graph networks excel at reasoning over relational state, their use has been predominantly discriminative. A generative graph network could synthesize high-fidelity, structured training data—such as object interaction sequences or scene dynamics—on demand, addressing a critical bottleneck in scalable agent training.
proposals.hypothesis: Graph networks, when formulated as autoregressive generative models of relational state, can produce structured synthetic data that preserves the underlying combinatorial and causal constraints of real-world environments, thereby improving the sample efficiency and generalization of downstream embodied agents.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-purpose graph network architecture, building on relational architectures from Wierstra's prior work. The model will learn an energy-based or flow-based generative process over the joint space of graph nodes (entities) and edges (relations). We will employ a training regime inspired by recent insights into grokking and double descent, using a combination of maximum likelihood on real graph-structured data (e.g., from SIMA's environment interactions) and adversarial or contrastive losses to ensure fidelity. The generation process will be autoregressive, sequentially sampling nodes and their relational edges.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for generating structured, relational data, directly applicable to training embodied agents and other reasoning systems. It will yield insights into the representational capacity of graph networks as generative models and offer a practical tool for mitigating data scarcity in complex simulation domains.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is inverting the standard discriminative application of graph networks for embodied reasoning into a controlled generative process, explicitly targeting the synthesis of data with complex relational constraints rather than simpler, unstructured outputs typical of prior generative models.

proposals.title: Adversarial Focus for Robust World Models: Preventing Catastrophic Delusion in Guided Model Learning
proposals.motivation: In model-based reinforcement learning, training a world model with a specific policy's experience can lead to catastrophic delusion, where the model overfits to the policy's trajectory distribution and forgets critical dynamics of the environment. This undermines the model's utility for planning, policy improvement, and transfer, which is a core challenge for generalist embodied agents like SIMA. A principled method is needed to allow a model to adapt to a policy's focus without losing its general predictive capacity.
proposals.hypothesis: Formalizing policy guidance as a learnable focus distribution over the state-action space, and adversarially constraining it via a 'capacity guardian' that enforces accuracy on a conserved buffer of empirical transitions, will yield world models that are both policy-relevant and robustly accurate, avoiding catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.method: We propose a minimax objective between three components: a policy network, a world model (e.g., a Graph Neural Network or Transformer for relational state), and an adversarial capacity guardian network. The policy induces a focus distribution, re-weighting the world model's training loss. The guardian's objective is to maximize a penalty proportional to the increase in the world model's prediction error on a fixed, conserved replay buffer. The world model and policy are trained to minimize policy cost subject to this adversarial penalty, using a Lagrangian or gradient reversal layer. This draws from pre-2026 adversarial regularization and distributionally robust optimization techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and practical algorithm for guided model learning that preserves general dynamics knowledge. It will contribute to more robust and sample-efficient model-based RL, directly applicable to training generalist agents in complex, relational virtual worlds. The analysis will also connect to phenomena like grokking and double descent in the context of constrained optimization.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training and experience replay are known, the novel integration of a learnable focus distribution with a dedicated adversarial 'guardian' explicitly tasked with preserving predictive capacity on a conserved buffer is a new formulation for preventing model delusion. This moves beyond simple buffer mixing or regularization.

proposals.title: Active World Models: Policy-Guided Active Learning for Task-Relevant Environment Dynamics
proposals.motivation: In model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL), learning a globally accurate world model is often sample-inefficient and unnecessary for task success. Pre-2026, methods like MuZero and Dreamer learn models from passive interaction data, but this can lead to wasted capacity on irrelevant dynamics. This work addresses the core challenge of sample efficiency in embodied agents (e.g., SIMA) by strategically focusing model capacity on task-critical aspects of the environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a policy can guide the learning of an environment model through active querying, prioritizing model fidelity on state-action transitions that are most uncertain or impactful for the task, leading to faster policy improvement and more sample-efficient world model learning than passive, uniform modeling.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-network architecture: a policy network and an environment model (e.g., a recurrent state-space model). The policy will output, alongside actions, an 'importance signal'—a scalar weight or a set of candidate queries—for each transition. This signal will be computed based on the policy's epistemic uncertainty (via an ensemble) and estimated value function sensitivity. The environment model's loss function will be augmented with these weights, creating a reweighted transition loss. We will test this within a modified DreamerV3-like framework, using relational networks to process the query structure, and evaluate in complex 3D simulated worlds.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formal framework for active, goal-directed environment model learning in RL, moving beyond passive experience replay. It will provide empirical evidence that task-aware model bias improves sample efficiency in long-horizon embodied tasks. The resulting 'active world model' paradigm could unify principles from adaptive experimental design with deep RL.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is inverting the standard MBRL loop: instead of the model guiding policy learning, the policy actively guides what the model learns. This is distinct from prior curiosity methods, as the guidance is explicitly tied to the policy's task-oriented uncertainty and long-horizon value, not generic prediction error.

proposals.title: Policy-Guided World Modeling: Inverting the Learning Objective for Efficient Embodied Reasoning
proposals.motivation: In model-based reinforcement learning for embodied agents (e.g., SIMA), a learned world model predicts future environment states, which a policy network then uses for planning. This forward pipeline can be inefficient, as the model learns a general transition function, while the policy may only need to understand specific, task-relevant aspects of the state. This misalignment wastes capacity and data, especially in complex, relational environments like 3D scenes where graph-based representations are prone to over-squashing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a policy network's latent representations and attention patterns can guide a world model to learn selectively, focusing its predictive capacity on state variables and relational structures that are most critical for decision-making, thereby improving sample efficiency and final task performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-network architecture where a relational graph neural network (GNN) policy, trained via online interaction in environments like those in SIMA, produces a saliency mask over the environment's state graph. This mask, derived from policy gradient magnitudes or attention heads, will dynamically re-weight the loss of a GNN-based world model, prioritizing accurate prediction of salient nodes and edges. The model's predictions are then fed back to the policy, creating a closed-loop, adaptive learning system. We will benchmark against standard model-based RL and fixed-graph methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for task-aware world modeling, demonstrating improved sample efficiency and robustness in embodied reasoning tasks. It will provide new insights into the coupling of representation learning and decision-making, potentially unifying principles from grokking, double descent, and graph processing.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is inverting the conventional objective: instead of the policy adapting to a fixed world model, the world model's learning objective is dynamically shaped by the policy's needs. This moves beyond static graph rewiring for over-squashing by allowing the policy to induce a soft, task-dependent graph structure for prediction.

proposals.title: Learning Task-Agnostic World Models for Imagination via Observation-Only Contrastive Objectives
proposals.motivation: Current approaches to learning world models for planning and imagination, such as Dreamer, are typically trained with access to environment reward signals, which ties the learned dynamics to a specific task. This limits the model's generality and its ability to be repurposed for new tasks without retraining. A model that learns a useful, task-agnostic imagination engine from pure observation streams would be a more fundamental and flexible component for generalist embodied agents, aligning with the long-term goal of open-ended learning in complex virtual worlds.
proposals.hypothesis: A relational neural network, trained via contrastive objectives on temporally extended observation sequences, can learn a latent state representation and transition dynamics that capture the causal and compositional structure of an environment, making it directly useful for downstream planning and reasoning tasks without ever seeing a reward signal.
proposals.method: We will extend the relational architectures explored in 'Leave Graphs Alone' to process scene observations, constructing implicit graphs of entities and their relations. The model will be trained using a multi-step contrastive predictive coding (CPC) objective, maximizing mutual information between the current latent state and future observations over extended horizons. We will integrate this with a variational autoencoder framework to learn a compact, disentangled state representation. The resulting model's utility will be evaluated by freezing its parameters and using its imagination (rollouts in latent space) as a substrate for a simple planner (e.g., Cross-Entropy Method) on novel downstream tasks in environments like the SIMA suite.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a method for learning general-purpose, reward-agnostic world models, a step toward more modular and reusable AI components. It will demonstrate how contrastive and relational inductive biases can capture task-relevant dynamics from observation alone, contributing to the unification of representation learning and model-based RL. The resulting models could serve as a foundational 'imagination engine' for future generalist agents.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is common in representation learning, its application to learning *action-conditioned* transition dynamics for multi-step imagination without any reward supervision is novel. The approach uniquely combines relational scene processing with long-horizon temporal contrastive objectives specifically for building a planning-ready world model.

proposals.title: Stochastic Hierarchical Imagination: Improving Model-Based RL with Structured Environment Models in Imagination-Augmented Agents
proposals.motivation: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) agents, like those using the Imagination-Augmented Agent (I2A) architecture, rely on learned environment models for planning. However, standard models are often deterministic or low-capacity, limiting their ability to capture the stochasticity and compositional structure of complex environments, such as the 3D worlds targeted by projects like SIMA. This mismatch can lead to poor generalization and brittle planning. Investigating richer learned models within the imagination core is crucial for building more robust and sample-efficient generalist agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating stochastic and hierarchically structured learned models into the I2A's imagination encoder will yield more robust, informative rollouts, leading to significantly improved policy performance and generalization in complex, partially observable environments compared to agents using standard deterministic models.
proposals.method: We will extend the I2A framework by replacing its standard environment model with a structured variational model. Specifically, we will implement a Hierarchical Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM) with stochastic latent variables at multiple timescales, drawing from pre-2026 world models literature. The imagination encoder will be adapted to process these richer, multi-level rollouts, potentially using relational networks or graph neural networks to reason over the hierarchical latent structure. Evaluation will be conducted in challenging, structured domains like MiniGrid, BabyAI, and a modified 3D simulation environment from the SIMA project suite.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework for integrating advanced stochastic and hierarchical world models into a leading model-based RL agent architecture. It will deliver empirical evidence on how model complexity within imagination rollouts affects final agent capabilities, particularly for generalization and long-horizon reasoning. The findings will inform the design of next-generation embodied agents for complex virtual worlds.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical and stochastic models exist, their integration and evaluation specifically within the I2A's imagination pathway—a key component for planning—is unexplored. The novelty lies in systematically combining these advanced model classes with the I2A's distinct 'imagination encoder' to test if richer internal simulation directly translates to better action selection in complex scenes.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Model-Based Imagination: Improving Data Efficiency in Embodied Agents via Self-Supervised Environment Models
proposals.motivation: Imagination-Augmented Agents (I2A) demonstrated the power of learned environment models for planning and data-efficient RL, but rely on costly, task-specific supervised learning of these models. In complex, open-ended virtual worlds like those targeted by SIMA, acquiring sufficient task-labeled data for model training is a major bottleneck. Integrating unsupervised or self-supervised model learning can decouple world understanding from specific tasks, enabling agents to build general, reusable world models from abundant unlabeled interaction data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a self-supervised, graph-structured world model, trained on raw agent experience without task rewards, into the I2A framework will significantly improve sample efficiency and generalization in embodied agents compared to I2A with task-supervised models.
proposals.method: We will extend the I2A architecture by replacing its supervised environment model with a relational neural network trained via contrastive predictive coding (CPC) on sequences of observations and actions. This model will learn to predict future latent states in a graph representation of the scene, capturing object and agent relations. The learned model will then serve as the 'imagination' module for planning, using Monte Carlo Tree Search over imagined trajectories. We will evaluate in 3D virtual environments (e.g., ProcGen, MiniGrid) and the Animal-AI testbed.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, more data-efficient paradigm for model-based RL in embodied AI, demonstrating how self-supervised learning can bootstrap world models for planning. It will provide empirical evidence on the transferability of unsupervised world models across tasks and offer insights into the relational structures necessary for effective imagination in complex scenes.
proposals.novelty: While I2A used supervised models and recent work explores unsupervised dynamics learning, their integration for *graph-structured imagination* in embodied agents is novel. This approach uniquely combines Wierstra's themes of learning, reasoning, and graphs with the SIMA agent's focus on generalist capability, moving toward agents that learn to reason without explicit task supervision.

proposals.title: Robust Meta-Imagination: Adversarial Meta-Learning of World Models for Out-of-Distribution Generalization
proposals.motivation: Generalist embodied agents like SIMA rely on learned world models for imagination-based planning, but their performance degrades catastrophically under distributional shifts in environment dynamics or task structure. This fragility limits their safe deployment in open-world, non-stationary settings. Meta-learning offers a path to learning adaptable imagination strategies, but current methods lack explicit robustness guarantees against worst-case perturbations.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-reinforcement learning framework, trained with adversarial environment perturbations and a co-adapted out-of-distribution (OOD) detector, can produce an imagination-based planning strategy that maintains robust performance and avoids catastrophic failure when faced with novel sensory and dynamic shifts.
proposals.method: We will extend MAML-style meta-learning to train a strategy network that parameterizes how to use a given world model for planning. The meta-training distribution will include procedurally generated tasks and adversarial perturbations (e.g., dynamics noise, visual corruptions) to maximize a robust objective. A lightweight OOD detection module, based on density estimation in the latent state space of the world model, will be co-trained meta-learned. Upon detecting novelty, this module triggers a fallback to a robust, exploration-heavy imagination protocol, also meta-learned.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a principled method for hardening model-based RL agents against distributional shift, moving beyond mere adaptation to include explicit OOD safety. It will provide new insights into the relationship between meta-learning, robustness, and world model reliability, with direct applications to more dependable embodied AI.
proposals.novelty: The integration of adversarial robustness objectives directly into the meta-learning of imagination strategies, coupled with a co-adapted, meta-learned OOD detection and fallback mechanism, is a novel synthesis beyond standard meta-RL or robust RL alone.

proposals.title: Generative Imagination for Relational State Prediction in Embodied Agents
proposals.motivation: Embodied agents like SIMA require robust world models to plan and act in complex, relational environments. Current methods for predicting future states often struggle with generating diverse, plausible outcomes from a given scene, limiting an agent's ability to imagine and reason about long-horizon consequences. This bottleneck is particularly acute in graph-structured state representations, where predicting node and edge dynamics is challenging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generative adversarial model, conditioned on a relational graph representation of the current state and an action, can produce a diverse set of plausible future state graphs that improve an agent's planning performance compared to deterministic or variational autoencoder-based predictors.
proposals.method: We will extend the agent's state representation as a scene graph, building on relational network architectures. A conditional Wasserstein GAN with gradient penalty (cWGAN-GP) will be trained to generate future graph states. The generator will take as input the current state graph and an action embedding, outputting a distribution over possible future graphs. The critic will learn to distinguish real from generated transitions while enforcing consistency with the action. Training will leverage environments from the SIMA framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel generative model for relational state prediction that explicitly captures transition uncertainty. It will provide a practical 'imagination' module for embodied agents, enabling more robust planning through sampling of diverse futures. We will also analyze the model's ability to mitigate over-squashing in graph-based predictions by learning to generate informative latent structures.
proposals.novelty: While GANs have been used for image-based future prediction, their application to generating structured, relational future states for embodied agents is novel. This approach moves beyond deterministic or VAE-based world models by explicitly adversarial training for diversity and plausibility in graph-structured outputs.

proposals.title: Isolating the Planning Core: A Component-Ablation Study of Imagination-Augmented Architectures in Structured MDPs
proposals.motivation: Imagination-augmented agents like I2A show promise for data-efficient learning through internal simulation, but their complexity makes it unclear which components are essential for planning benefit. This opacity hinders principled scaling and deployment in real-world systems like embodied agents (e.g., SIMA) where sample efficiency is critical. A rigorous, component-level analysis is needed to move beyond monolithic architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: The planning benefit of I2A-like architectures in structured environments stems not from the full ensemble of learned models, but from a minimal core component—specifically, the aggregation mechanism that selectively integrates simulated trajectories—while a simple, non-learned rollout policy can suffice.
proposals.method: We will construct a suite of simple, procedurally generated grid-world MDPs with sparse rewards and long time horizons, where a PPO baseline learns slowly. We implement a modular I2A variant where the environment model (a learned transition function), rollout policy (learned or random), value predictor, and aggregation network (e.g., a relational graph attention module) can be independently ablated or replaced with naive versions.
