We will measure the marginal data-efficiency gain of each component and their combinations across the environmental difficulty gradient.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative isolation of planning components in model-based RL, yielding a clear recipe for building minimally complex yet effective planning agents. The resulting benchmark and ablation framework will serve as a diagnostic tool for future architecture design, directly informing sample-efficient training for complex domains like scene understanding and embodied reasoning.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats I2A as an indivisible unit, we deconstruct it to identify the minimal sufficient conditions for planning. This moves beyond comparing monolithic agents to baselines and instead performs causal, within-architecture analysis of sub-module contributions.

proposals.title: Optimal Foraging for Thought: A Minimalist Persist-or-Pivot Mechanism for Imagination-Augmented Agents
proposals.motivation: Imagination-Augmented Agents (I2A) demonstrate strong performance in planning but are computationally expensive, relying on deep model-based rollouts. This complexity limits their scalability and obscures the core mechanisms behind their benefit. A minimalist, interpretable theory of when and how to use 'imagination' is needed to build more efficient and generalist agents, aligning with the push toward simpler, more robust architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary benefit of I2A can be captured by a minimalist, Optimal Foraging Theory-inspired arbitration mechanism that decides, based on a crude value estimate, whether to persist with the current action sequence or pivot to a new subgoal, maintaining performance on simple tasks with drastically reduced complexity.
proposals.method: We will design an agent with a standard policy/value network (e.g., IMPALA) and a lightweight, learned transition model. Instead of full rollouts, the model will generate a single-step 'patch value' estimate for a candidate subgoal. An OFT-inspired rule, formalized as a switching threshold comparing the current action sequence's discounted value against the estimated opportunity cost (travel + handling time to the new subgoal), will govern arbitration. We will test this on grid-world foraging and navigation tasks from the MiniGrid and BabyAI suites, benchmarking against a full I2A and a model-free baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, bio-inspired formalization of a core planning operation (persist-or-pivot) and deliver a drastically simplified I2A variant. It will contribute to the understanding of which components of complex architectures are essential, promoting more efficient and interpretable agent design for embodied AI.
proposals.novelty: Applying Optimal Foraging Theory's parsimonious decision rules to the internal 'foraging' of an agent's imagination process is a novel cross-disciplinary analogy. It directly challenges the need for expensive rollouts by reducing imagination to a sparse, arbitration-focused signal.

proposals.title: Learning to Attend: Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Attention Policies in Sequential Generation
proposals.motivation: Current autoregressive models, including transformers, rely on fixed attention patterns or heuristics, which may be suboptimal for complex, long-horizon generation tasks like scene composition or multi-step reasoning. This limits their efficiency and ability to dynamically manage computational resources. An adaptive attention mechanism, learned to optimize task performance, could significantly improve the capabilities of generative agents in embodied and relational settings.
proposals.hypothesis: An attention policy, trained via reinforcement learning to select which parts of an internal state or external environment to attend to next, will outperform static or self-supervised attention mechanisms in terms of sample efficiency and final task performance in sequential generation domains.
proposals.method: We propose a two-level architecture where a base model (e.g., a transformer or graph network) performs generation, and a lightweight RL agent (using an algorithm like PPO or an actor-critic method) learns a policy over discrete attention actions. The policy's state will be a compressed representation of the generation history and current context. Rewards will be sparse, task-completion signals (e.g., from a simulator like in SIMA) combined with dense penalties for computational steps, encouraging efficient attention. We will test this in virtual 3D scene building and multi-hop relational reasoning tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for integrating RL into the core attention mechanism of generative models, moving beyond training the model weights. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of learned attention policies for sample efficiency in embodied agents and complex reasoning. The resulting method could offer a principled alternative to address over-squashing in graphs by learning dynamic, task-relevant connectivity.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been used for architecture search and discrete latent variable models, applying it directly to learn a real-time, sequential policy for attention *during* generation is a distinct and unexplored direction. This unifies the themes of learning state-based policies (from SIMA) with adaptive relational reasoning over graph-structured information.

proposals.title: Memory-Augmented Scene Generation: A Relational Neural Architecture for Compositional Image Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Current autoregressive image generation models, such as those based on transformers, often struggle with long-range coherence and compositional reasoning, leading to inconsistent object attributes and spatial relationships. This limits their application in generating complex, multi-object scenes from detailed textual descriptions. Drawing inspiration from human memory's ability to store and recombine perceptual parts, we propose a novel architecture to address these limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting a generative model with a differentiable, relational memory system, inspired by human episodic and semantic memory, will enable more coherent and controllable generation of complex scenes by explicitly storing and retrieving reusable visual concepts (parts, objects, styles).
proposals.method: We will develop a relational memory-augmented neural network (RMNN) for image generation. The core is a key-value memory matrix where keys encode relational graph embeddings of scene descriptions (objects, attributes, relations), and values store corresponding latent feature maps of image parts. During generation, a transformer-based controller will query this memory using the current scene graph state, retrieved features will be integrated via adaptive instance normalization, and the memory will be updated with new compositions. We will train the system end-to-end on datasets like Visual Genome and CLEVR.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new neuro-symbolic architecture for image synthesis that explicitly bridges high-level relational reasoning with low-level pixel generation. It will provide empirical evidence for the benefits of structured, external memory in generative tasks and deliver a model capable of more faithful and editable scene construction compared to monolithic transformers.
proposals.novelty: While memory-augmented networks exist (e.g., Neural Turing Machines), their application to generative modeling of complex scenes is novel. The key innovation is the use of a *relational* memory structure, informed by scene graphs and human memory models, to guide the compositional generation process, moving beyond simple sequence modeling.

proposals.title: One-Shot Adaptation via State-Space World Models and Relational Scene Priors
proposals.motivation: Current embodied agents, like SIMA, require extensive meta-training on vast datasets of procedurally generated tasks to achieve one-shot adaptation. This is computationally prohibitive and limits deployment to novel environments. The field needs agents that can adapt to unseen tasks without this heavy prior exposure, moving closer to human-like compositional reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: An agent equipped with a structured, relational world model and strong priors about physical and social scene dynamics can perform one-shot adaptation to novel tasks without a dedicated meta-training phase, by leveraging general-purpose reasoning over its internal state-space.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-architecture agent. First, a relational graph neural network (GNN) builds a persistent, object-centric scene graph from visual input, informed by priors from large-scale static scene datasets (e.g., Matterport3D). Second, a recurrent state-space model (SSM) learns to predict future graph states and rewards from actions, trained purely on passive exploration in diverse, non-curated environments. Adaptation is framed as Bayesian inference within this joint model: a single demonstration updates the belief state over task goals, which the SSM uses for planning.
proposals.contribution: This work would demonstrate a new paradigm for sample-efficient adaptation, decoupling task learning from prior task-specific experience. It contributes a novel architecture combining relational scene understanding with state-space dynamics, and provides empirical evidence challenging the necessity of large-scale meta-training for one-shot learning in embodied AI.
proposals.novelty: Instead of meta-learning a policy, we meta-learn a structured, factorized world model and reasoning process. The approach unifies Wierstra's themes of state-space models, relational reasoning, and scene understanding to bypass traditional meta-training, drawing inspiration from 'Leave Graphs Alone' for robust graph processing and 'Unifying Grokking' for understanding generalization phases.

proposals.title: Unbounded Task Streams: Lifelong Learning for Embodied Agents via Relational State-Space Models
proposals.motivation: Current embodied AI agents, like SIMA, are trained on static, curated datasets of tasks, which fails to capture the open-ended, non-stationary nature of real-world interaction. This static paradigm limits an agent's ability to adapt to novel challenges and leads to catastrophic forgetting. Addressing this is crucial for developing truly generalist agents that can operate in dynamic, unbounded virtual worlds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agent equipped with a relational, state-space world model can learn and reason over a non-stationary, unbounded stream of tasks without catastrophic forgetting, by dynamically expanding its internal graph representation of skills and environment dynamics.
proposals.method: We will extend the SIMA 2 architecture with a core relational state-space model (RSSM) that maintains a dynamic, unbounded graph of learned skills, object affordances, and environment states. Inspired by 'Leave Graphs Alone', we will use a task-conditioned attention mechanism over this graph to retrieve and compose skills for novel tasks, avoiding over-squashing. A novelty-driven intrinsic reward, modulated by the model's prediction error, will guide exploration in the unbounded task stream, promoting continual learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for lifelong learning in embodied agents, moving beyond static task distributions. It will provide new insights into managing non-stationarity in reinforcement learning and demonstrate how relational reasoning can be scaled to unbounded, open-ended problem spaces.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in unifying the relational, graph-based reasoning from Wierstra's recent work with the challenge of unbounded task streams, proposing a dynamic graph expansion mechanism for continual learning instead of static architectures or fixed rewiring schemes.

proposals.title: Learning to Manage Memory: A Reinforcement Learning Approach for Adaptive Information Retention in Graph-Structured State Spaces
proposals.motivation: Current neural architectures, especially in embodied agents and relational reasoning tasks, struggle with long-term credit assignment and catastrophic forgetting when processing sequential, structured information. This is a core bottleneck for agents like SIMA operating in complex virtual worlds, where efficient memory is crucial for planning and learning. Explicitly learning a memory management policy could address these issues, moving beyond fixed heuristics.
proposals.hypothesis: An agent can learn an effective, generalizable policy for writing, retaining, and forgetting information using reinforcement learning, where the reward is derived from the downstream utility of the memory contents for task performance, leading to more efficient and robust long-term reasoning.
proposals.method: We propose a two-level architecture: a core task network (e.g., a GNN or transformer for relational state) and a differentiable external memory module. An RL agent, using an algorithm like PPO, learns to output discrete write/retain/forget actions for memory slots based on the current state and memory content. The reward signal is a combination of task success and a sparsity penalty on memory usage. We will test this in environments requiring long-horizon reasoning, such as modified BabyAI or graph-based navigation tasks, building on insights from relational networks and the analysis of learning dynamics in over-squashed graphs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, learned alternative to static memory architectures, potentially improving sample efficiency and generalization in embodied agents. It will also yield interpretable insights into what information an agent deems valuable to retain over time, linking memory management to task structure.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been used for many control tasks, applying it explicitly to manage an internal, structured memory buffer as a discrete action space is underexplored. This approach unifies the 'learning to learn' perspective with explicit memory mechanisms, moving beyond gradient-based memory updates or fixed attention.

proposals.title: Inverse Memory Networks: One-Shot Data Generation via Differentiable Memory Inversion
proposals.motivation: Current generative models require vast datasets for training, limiting their applicability in data-scarce domains like embodied AI or complex reasoning tasks. A method to generate diverse, structured samples from a single example would dramatically improve sample efficiency and enable rapid adaptation. This directly addresses a core challenge in training generalist agents like SIMA, which must learn from limited interactions in diverse virtual worlds.
proposals.hypothesis: The structured, relational memory of a trained memory-augmented network (MANN) encodes a compressed, disentangled representation of a data distribution, and this representation can be probabilistically inverted to generate novel, valid samples consistent with the original distribution from just one seed example.
proposals.method: We will build upon relational memory core architectures and differentiable neural computers (pre-2026). The core technical innovation is a two-phase process: 1) Train a MANN on a target domain (e.g., graph-structured scenes or action sequences) to perform a prediction or completion task, forcing it to learn a generalizable state representation. 2) Fix the MANN's memory and weights, and treat the memory write operations as a generative prior. We will train a lightweight 'inversion network' that, conditioned on a single novel input, learns to propose perturbations to the memory read/write heads and latent state to produce diverse, plausible outputs through the frozen forward network, regularized by the MANN's own internal consistency measures.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel paradigm for one-shot or few-shot data generation, moving beyond traditional likelihood-based generative models. It will provide a new theoretical lens on the content of learned memory structures and demonstrate practical utility for generating synthetic training data for downstream tasks in graph learning and scene understanding, directly benefiting sample-efficient reinforcement learning.
proposals.novelty: While memory networks are used for recall and reasoning, using their internal dynamics in reverse as a structured generative engine is unexplored. This contrasts with standard GANs/VAEs and connects memory-augmentation to generative modeling in a non-obvious way, leveraging the relational biases learned for reasoning to constrain generation.

proposals.title: A Unified State-Space Model for Cross-Domain One-Shot Adaptation via Relational Scene Graphs
proposals.motivation: Current AI systems, including generalist embodied agents like SIMA, require extensive task-specific training or fine-tuning, limiting their adaptability to novel domains. Achieving robust one-shot learning across vastly different modalities (e.g., virtual 3D environments, symbolic reasoning tasks, and natural language instructions) remains a core challenge for artificial general intelligence. This problem is exacerbated by architectural biases and over-squashing in graph-based models when handling new relational structures.
proposals.hypothesis: A single model, built upon a relational state-space architecture that explicitly separates domain-agnostic dynamics from task-specific structure, can perform one-shot adaptation to novel domains by inferring and integrating a minimal task graph from a single demonstration.
proposals.method: We propose a novel architecture centered on a learnable, factorized state representation. A core relational network, inspired by work on addressing over-squashing without rewiring, will maintain a persistent, task-agnostic world model. For a new domain, a single demonstration will be parsed into a scene graph, which is then fused with the core state via a lightweight, attention-based adapter module. This approach builds upon the SIMA 2 paradigm but replaces its multi-task training with a structured inference mechanism, enabling the model to 'bootstrap' a task representation without weight updates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete architecture and training paradigm for cross-domain one-shot learning, moving beyond multi-task generalists to true adaptive systems. It will offer new insights into the unification of learning (from data) and reasoning (over graphs) within a single stateful model. The resulting benchmark and analysis will delineate the limits of adaptation without retraining.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit factorization of persistent world dynamics from inferred task graphs, enabling one-shot adaptation without architectural rewiring or gradient updates. This contrasts with prior multi-task models (like SIMA) that learn a fixed set of skills and with meta-learning approaches that require inner-loop optimization on new tasks.

proposals.title: Curriculum-Driven Episodic Sampling for Generalist Embodied Agents
proposals.motivation: The training of generalist embodied agents like SIMA 2 relies on vast, diverse datasets of interaction episodes. Naive uniform sampling of these episodes is inefficient, leading to slow convergence and poor generalization to novel tasks and environments. A principled strategy for sampling training episodes is a critical, underexplored lever for improving the sample efficiency and final performance of such agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically constructing a curriculum over the space of training episodes—based on learned measures of agent competence and environmental complexity—will significantly improve the generalization and convergence speed of embodied agents compared to standard uniform or random sampling.
proposals.method: We will develop a relational graph representation of the episodic dataset, where nodes are episodes and edges encode relational similarities in task semantics, state-space complexity, and required skill composition. A meta-controller network will learn to sample from this graph, guided by a curriculum objective that balances the agent's learning progress (inspired by competence-based methods) with coverage of the relational structure. This sampling policy will be trained jointly with the main agent using a bi-level optimization framework, updating the sampling strategy based on the agent's validation performance on held-out task distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, theoretically-grounded framework for episodic curriculum learning in reinforcement learning. It will deliver a scalable method to improve the sample efficiency of large-scale agent training, validated within the SIMA framework, and offer new insights into the relationship between data ordering, relational structure, and generalization.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning is established, applying it at the granularity of episodic sampling for embodied agents via a learned relational graph is novel. This directly addresses over-squashing in the training pipeline by structuring the temporal flow of experience, complementing prior graph-based work on network architectures.

proposals.title: Learning to Learn Episodes: Meta-Reinforcement Learning for Curriculum Generation in Embodied Agents
proposals.motivation: Training generalist embodied agents like SIMA requires vast, diverse datasets of expert episodes, which are expensive to collect and may not optimally cover the skill progression needed for efficient learning. Current methods rely on heuristics or uniform sampling for episode selection, which can lead to suboptimal training efficiency and poor generalization. Automating the selection and construction of training curricula is a critical, unsolved problem for scaling embodied AI.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-reinforcement learning agent, operating over a graph-structured representation of the agent's state and the relational structure of the environment, can learn to generate or select training episodes that maximize the learning progress of a target embodied agent, leading to faster skill acquisition and better final performance.
proposals.method: We propose a bi-level framework. The outer loop is a meta-RL policy (e.g., a recurrent or transformer-based network) that observes the target agent's learning state (performance, uncertainty, skill graph) and the relational graph of available or composable environment episodes. Its action is to select or sequence a training episode. The inner loop trains the target agent (e.g., a SIMA-like model) on the selected episode. The meta-agent's reward is a function of the target agent's improvement on a held-out validation set of tasks. We will leverage relational networks and graph neural networks to process the state and environment structure, building on Wierstra's work on relational reasoning and graph-based methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, data-driven method for automatic curriculum generation in embodied AI, moving beyond hand-designed curricula. It will yield insights into the relationship between task structure, learning dynamics, and optimal training sequences, contributing to theories of efficient learning in complex environments.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning and automated task generation exist, applying meta-RL to learn a dynamic, graph-conditioned policy for episode selection specifically for embodied agents is novel. This integrates Wierstra's themes of learning, reasoning, and graphs directly into the training loop of agents like SIMA, rather than just their architecture.

proposals.title: Relational Support Set Reasoning with Graph Neural Networks for Few-Shot Generalization
proposals.motivation: In meta-learning and few-shot learning, the support set is a critical source of context for task adaptation, but standard methods (e.g., metric-based or optimization-based) often treat examples independently or aggregate them via simple pooling, neglecting rich relational structure. This limits an agent's ability to perform complex relational reasoning and generalize from sparse data, a key challenge for embodied agents like SIMA operating in diverse virtual worlds. Explicitly modeling the support set's relational graph could address this, improving sample efficiency and task comprehension.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the support set as a graph and processing it with a Graph Neural Network (GNN) will enable more effective relational reasoning, leading to superior few-shot generalization and task performance compared to methods that treat support examples independently.
proposals.method: We will frame the support set for a given task as a graph, where nodes are embedded examples (e.g., via a pretrained encoder) and edges are constructed based on semantic or task-specific similarity metrics. A relational GNN (e.g., a Graph Attention Network or Message Passing Neural Network) will process this graph to produce refined, context-aware embeddings for each support example. These relational embeddings will then be integrated into a prototypical network or a memory-augmented neural network architecture for final prediction. We will benchmark against standard few-shot baselines on relational reasoning datasets (e.g., CLEVR) and within embodied environments inspired by the SIMA framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, graph-structured approach to support set processing that explicitly captures inter-example relations for few-shot learning. It will provide empirical evidence on how relational reasoning over context improves generalization. The resulting architecture could serve as a plug-in module for existing meta-learning and embodied agent systems, enhancing their ability to learn from limited, structured data.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs are established for relational reasoning, their application to explicitly model the relational graph *within* a support set for meta-learning is novel. This moves beyond using graphs for input data (e.g., scene graphs) or for neural network rewiring, instead focusing on the relational structure of the contextual examples themselves to guide adaptation.

proposals.title: Zero-Shot Scene Graph Generation via Relational State Abstraction and Language Grounding
proposals.motivation: Current scene graph generation models require extensive labeled training data for each predicate and object class, limiting their adaptability to novel environments or tasks. This is a critical bottleneck for creating generalist embodied agents, like SIMA, that must operate in diverse, unseen virtual worlds. Enabling zero-shot relational reasoning, where no visual examples of a relation are provided, is essential for scalable and flexible scene understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly separating the learning of a generic, compositional relational state space from the grounding of natural language descriptions to that space, we can achieve zero-shot generalization to unseen visual relations without task-specific fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will build upon recent work on relational networks and state-based abstractions. The model will consist of a visual encoder that extracts object features and a graph neural network that learns to compose them into a structured, abstract relational state, addressing over-squashing via methods from 'Leave Graphs Alone'. A separate, pre-trained language model will project textual relation descriptions (e.g., 'holding', 'next to') into the same abstract state space. Zero-shot prediction is performed by matching the language-derived state template to the most compatible compositional state derived from the visual scene.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for zero-shot visual relation learning, directly advancing the scene understanding capabilities of embodied agents. It will yield a benchmark and methodology for evaluating compositional generalization in scene graphs, bridging the fields of relational reasoning, graph representation learning, and language-vision integration.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior meta or few-shot learning approaches that still require some support examples, this method aims for true zero-shot capability by leveraging the compositional structure of the relational state space itself. It uniquely combines insights from graph representation learning (over-squashing) and generalist agent research (SIMA) to tackle the problem.

proposals.title: Scalable Bayesian Deep Learning for Relational Scene Understanding in Embodied Agents
proposals.motivation: Bayes by Backprop (BBB) provides a principled framework for uncertainty quantification in neural networks, a critical feature for safe and robust decision-making in embodied AI. However, its application has been largely confined to small-scale models, limiting its utility for complex tasks like relational reasoning in 3D scenes, a core theme in Daan Wierstra's research. Scaling BBB to modern architectures would bridge the gap between Bayesian deep learning and practical, large-scale applications in virtual worlds.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating structured, factorized posterior approximations and leveraging insights from the unification of grokking and double descent, Bayes by Backprop can be scaled effectively to large graph neural networks and transformer-based architectures without catastrophic performance or memory overhead.
proposals.method: We will extend BBB using a structured variational posterior over weights, employing low-rank or Kronecker-factored Gaussian approximations to manage parameter count. We will integrate this into relational architectures, such as Graph Networks used in scene understanding and the agent networks from SIMA 2, focusing on the learning dynamics at scale. The training will be analyzed through the lens of grokking and double descent phenomena to identify stable scaling regimes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, scalable Bayesian deep learning method for large networks, enabling robust uncertainty estimation in embodied agents for 3D environments. It will provide empirical evidence on the learning dynamics of Bayesian neural networks at scale, connecting to broader phenomena in deep learning theory.
proposals.novelty: While BBB is well-studied, its application to large-scale, relational architectures for embodied scene understanding remains unexplored. The novelty lies in combining scalable variational inference with architectures central to recent agent research, guided by theoretical insights into generalization phenomena.

proposals.title: Distributed Variational Inference for Large Bayesian Neural Networks via Graph-Parameterized Ensembles
proposals.motivation: Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) provide principled uncertainty quantification crucial for safe and reliable AI in embodied agents and reasoning systems. However, scaling variational inference (VI) to massive networks and datasets is computationally prohibitive on single devices, limiting their application to complex tasks like scene understanding and relational reasoning. A distributed training scheme is needed to unlock the potential of large-scale BNNs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed VI scheme, which leverages a graph-structured ensemble of local variational posteriors coordinated via a lightweight relational state, can efficiently approximate the true posterior of a massive BNN while being robust to communication bottlenecks and device heterogeneity.
proposals.method: We will develop a method where the global BNN is partitioned across devices, each maintaining a local variational posterior over its subset of parameters. Inspired by relational networks and graph neural networks, a small, shared 'coordination graph' will model dependencies between partitions, enabling efficient, low-bandwidth synchronization of beliefs. Training will use a distributed, amortized version of the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO), leveraging techniques from SIMA's asynchronous multi-agent training and addressing over-squashing in the coordination graph's message-passing steps.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical framework for training billion-parameter BNNs, directly advancing probabilistic deep learning. It will provide theoretical insights into the factorization of variational distributions in distributed systems and demonstrate empirical gains in uncertainty calibration for large-scale tasks in virtual environments and graph-based reasoning.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior distributed VI work focused on data parallelism, our approach introduces model parallelism with a learned, graph-based relational state for coordinating parameter uncertainties, merging insights from distributed systems, graph representation learning, and variational inference in a novel way.

proposals.title: Temporal Scene Graphs: Learning Relational World Models from Unlabeled Video Sequences
proposals.motivation: Current scene understanding models are predominantly trained on static images, which fails to capture the rich temporal dynamics and causal relationships inherent in real-world environments. For embodied agents like SIMA, reasoning about object permanence, state transitions, and multi-step interactions is crucial for robust planning and action. Learning these dynamics from passive video observation, before any agent interaction, could provide a powerful, data-efficient prior for downstream reasoning and control tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A model trained to predict future scene graph states from video sequences will learn a more robust and generalizable relational world model than one trained on static frames, leading to improved performance in tasks requiring temporal and causal reasoning.
proposals.method: We propose a self-supervised learning framework where a video encoder (e.g., a Transformer or a Temporal Convolutional Network) processes sequences of raw video frames. The model's objective is to predict the future state of a scene graph, represented as a set of objects, their attributes, and their relational edges, using a relational network or graph neural network decoder. This builds on Wierstra's work on relational networks and addresses over-squashing by employing recent graph propagation techniques without rewiring. The training data will consist of large-scale, unlabeled video datasets (e.g., Ego4D, Something-Something) paired with automatically extracted, noisy initial scene graphs from pre-2026 object detectors and relationship predictors.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel method for learning structured, temporal world models directly from video, moving beyond static perception. It will provide empirical evidence for the value of temporal pre-training for embodied AI, specifically evaluating improvements in simulation-based planning and few-shot action prediction tasks. The resulting model could serve as a foundational perceptual module for next-generation agents like SIMA.
proposals.novelty: While scene graph generation from images is established, learning to predict their temporal evolution from raw video in a self-supervised manner is a nascent and non-trivial challenge. The key novelty lies in unifying the temporal modeling of high-dimensional pixel data with the structured prediction of discrete, relational graph states, creating a bridge between dense perception and abstract reasoning.

proposals.title: Scene Graph Meta-Learning for One-Shot Adaptation in Embodied Agents
proposals.motivation: Generalist embodied agents like SIMA 2 excel in diverse virtual worlds but require extensive training data for each new scene type, limiting their adaptability. Enabling rapid learning from minimal examples is crucial for real-world deployment where environments are constantly changing. This work addresses the core challenge of sample-efficient scene understanding for embodied AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by meta-learning a relational scene graph prior over diverse environments, an agent can adapt its internal world model to novel scene types from a single demonstration, significantly improving task performance in few-shot settings.
proposals.method: We will extend the SIMA 2 architecture with a meta-learning framework. The agent's internal state will be structured as a relational graph, informed by the 'Leave Graphs Alone' principles to preserve long-range dependencies. Using a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) objective, we will train the agent to rapidly adapt its graph-based scene representation from a single demonstration (support set) and then execute tasks (query set) in procedurally generated 3D environments. The relational reasoning module will be fine-tuned on the support set's scene graph.
proposals.contribution: This will yield a concrete method for one-shot scene adaptation in embodied agents, demonstrating significant performance gains over fine-tuning baselines. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating few-shot learning in 3D interactive environments and provide insights into the relationship between graph-structured representations and meta-learning efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning and graph networks are established, their integration for one-shot adaptation of an embodied agent's *scene model* is novel. This approach uniquely combines the relational reasoning of scene graphs with the rapid adaptation of meta-learning, moving beyond task-level to scene-level generalization.

ideas.title: On-Policy DDPG via Immune System-Inspired Clonal Selection
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that DDPG requires an off-policy replay buffer for stable learning. Drawing an analogy from immunology's clonal selection theory, which explains efficient, on-demand adaptation without a comprehensive historical archive, we propose a novel on-policy algorithm. The method replaces the replay buffer with a population of actor-critic networks. After gathering a batch of fresh experience under the current policy, the agent evaluates which population members (clones) perform best on this specific data. It then selectively updates the policy by amplifying these high-performing clones and applying targeted parameter mutations for exploration, effectively performing a form of on-policy population-based policy gradient. This aims to match the sample efficiency of buffer-based methods by focusing learning resources exclusively on relevant, current experience, eliminating the stability issues and off-policy corrections associated with replay.

ideas.title: Adversarial Information-Preserving Model Focus
ideas.description: This redesigned system formalizes the policy's guidance as a learnable 'focus distribution' over the state-action space for the environment model's training. To prevent the policy from causing the model to forget critical dynamics, an adversarial component, the capacity guardian, is tasked with ensuring the model's predictions on a conserved buffer of empirical transitions remain accurate. The learning objective becomes a minimax game: the policy minimizes its own expected cost subject to the model's focused predictions, while the guardian maximizes a penalty if the model's accuracy on the conserved buffer degrades. This ensures the model adapts to the policy's needs without losing general predictive capability, leading to robust, guided model learning that avoids catastrophic delusion.

ideas.title: Stabilized On-Policy Deep RL via Decoupled Successor Features and Policy Regularization
ideas.description: This system eliminates the conventional replay buffer by structuring learning into two decoupled components: a slow, amortized successor feature network and a fast, regularized on-policy actor. The successor feature network learns a policy-agnostic representation of the environment's dynamics and reward structure from a rolling window of recent policy data, providing a stable baseline for value estimation. The actor is trained on-policy using this stabilized critic, with an added divergence constraint that limits how far the new policy can stray from the state distribution induced by recent policies. This architecture maintains sample efficiency by reusing data within the short window for representation learning while ensuring convergence by preventing the destructive feedback loops inherent in purely on-policy value estimation.

ideas.title: Interference-Robust Multi-Physics Policy via Gradient Surgery and Modular Adaptation
ideas.description: This system learns a single policy for multiple physics tasks while explicitly combating catastrophic interference. It employs a base neural network with a shared feature backbone and task-conditional policy outputs. During training, a gradient projection technique resolves conflicts between tasks before updating the shared parameters. Furthermore, a lightweight task-identification module or gating network allows the system to specialize sub-networks for different physics domains, enabling positive transfer where tasks are similar while preventing destructive updates where they are not.

ideas.title: Component-Ablation I2A for Isolating Planning Benefit in Minimally Complex Environments
ideas.description: This research designs a suite of simple but structured environments where a reactive agent can eventually learn a policy, but does so with high sample complexity. Against this benchmark, a modular I2A-like architecture is constructed where core components—the learned environment model, the rollout policy, the value prediction, and the aggregation mechanism—can be independently ablated. The study rigorously measures the marginal data-efficiency gain contributed by each component across the difficulty gradient, precisely identifying the minimal set required for planning benefit to emerge beyond model-free baselines and providing a clear pathway for scaling component complexity.

ideas.title: Policy-Guided Active Learning for Environment Models
ideas.description: This idea inverts the standard model-based RL paradigm by having the policy network guide what the environment model should learn, inspired by goal-oriented active learning in scientific computing. The analogous solution involves adaptive experimental design, where data collection is strategically guided by a final objective rather than aiming for uniform model fidelity. Adapted to RL, the policy would generate 'queries' or 'importance weights' highlighting environment dynamics it deems most valuable or uncertain for its own task performance. The environment model's learning objective is then augmented to prioritize accuracy on these policy-specified regions of the state-action space, creating a synergistic loop where the policy actively shapes the model's development towards task-relevant mastery.

ideas.title: A Multi-Physics Policy via Modular Skill Composition
ideas.description: This idea challenges the single-task learning assumption in DDPG by drawing an analogy from Evolutionary Developmental Biology. In Evo-Devo, complex forms arise from the recombination of conserved genetic modules, not task-specific blueprints. The adapted solution is to modify DDPG to learn not a single monolithic policy, but a shared library of reusable, primitive skill modules (low-level policy components) across multiple physics tasks. A meta-controller or composer network then learns to dynamically assemble and sequence these modules into a coherent policy for any specific task. This allows a single agent to generalize across diverse physics domains by composing known skills in novel ways, mirroring how biological systems achieve adaptability through modularity and reuse.

ideas.title: Robust Meta-Learning of Imagination Strategies with Distributional Shift Resilience
ideas.description: This research designs a meta-learning framework that not only learns how to best use an imagination model for task-solving but is explicitly hardened against distributional shift. The meta-training process incorporates adversarial environment perturbations and a broad spectrum of procedurally generated tasks to optimize for robust, worst-case imagination strategy performance. A key component is a lightweight OOD detection module co-trained with the strategy, which triggers a fallback to a more conservative, exploration-heavy imagination protocol when novelty is detected. This results in a meta-learned imagination strategy that maintains reliable performance and avoids catastrophic failure when deployed in novel or non-stationary environments.

ideas.title: Recurrent DDPG for Partial Observability with Implicit Belief State Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a Recurrent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (RDDPG) algorithm designed for continuous control under partial observability. The core innovation is the replacement of the standard feed-forward actor and critic networks with recurrent neural networks that maintain a persistent hidden state, enabling the agent to accumulate evidence and infer latent environmental state over time. This approach allows the agent to learn effective policies directly from partial observation sequences without requiring an explicit, hand-designed state estimator. The resulting agent is more sample-efficient and robust in non-Markovian environments compared to memoryless DDPG variants operating on raw partial observations.

ideas.title: DDPG with Online Domain Adaptation for Safe and Efficient Real-World Robotic Policy Transfer
ideas.description: This research develops a modified DDPG architecture that explicitly bridges the simulation-to-reality gap for robotic control. The system pre-trains a policy in simulation but augments it with an online adaptation module that learns a real-world dynamics residual during initial, constrained real-world execution. This allows the policy to continuously compensate for unmodeled dynamics and sensorimotor delays, achieving robust performance on the physical robot. The approach is validated on real-world robotic manipulation tasks, measuring both final performance and the sample efficiency/safety of the adaptation process compared to direct transfer or pure real-world training.

ideas.title: Minimalist I2A via Ecological Foraging Theory
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Behavioral Ecology—Optimal Foraging Theory (OFT)—to the problem of designing a minimalist Imagination-Augmented Agent (I2A). OFT successfully explains complex animal foraging decisions using drastically simplified models based on a few key variables (e.g., travel time, handling time). Similarly, we propose to strip the I2A architecture down to its minimal beneficial components by implementing an OFT-inspired decision rule. The agent will use a crude, model-based 'imagination' step not for full rollouts, but to estimate a simple opportunity cost: the predicted value of persisting in the current action sequence versus the cost of switching to a new subgoal. This creates a minimalist I2A variant that tests whether the core benefit of I2A lies in this basic 'persist-or-pivot' arbitration mechanism, dramatically reducing computational complexity while maintaining performance on simple tasks.

ideas.title: Adaptive Action Clipping via Physiological Homeostasis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of physiological homeostasis to solve the problem of unknown or dynamically changing action bounds in DDPG. In systems physiology, organisms like mammals regulate variables (e.g., blood pressure) without knowing the precise environmental disturbances by using feedback loops that adapt effector response ranges. The analogous solution involves sensorimotor loops with saturating nonlinearities and adaptive setpoints. Adapted to DDPG, the actor network would output an unbounded signal, which is then passed through a parameterized, saturating output function (e.g., a shifted and scaled tanh). A separate, lightweight adaptive module, trained via an auxiliary loss from the critic, would dynamically adjust the saturation limits and center point of this function. This allows the agent to discover and track the valid action space through experience, effectively learning its own 'action homeostasis' without the assumption of known, fixed bounds.

ideas.title: Compositional Imagination via Program-Guided Generative Models
ideas.description: This research direction combines program synthesis with deep generative models to create a structured imagination engine. A program induction module generates interpretable, compositional procedures that specify the steps and constraints for imagining future states. These procedures then guide and condition a generative adversarial model or VAE, which renders the high-dimensional, plausible outcomes. This synergy yields an imagination system where the 'how' and 'why' of a generated scenario are explicitly represented as a program, enabling counterfactual editing, causal analysis, and the transfer of learned imaginative subroutines to novel tasks.

ideas.title: Symbolic Imagination from Observation: Learning and Reasoning Without Reward
ideas.description: This research direction aims to learn a world model purely from observational sequences, but instead of outputting high-dimensional reconstructions, it learns to generate and refine symbolic state abstractions and transition rules. These learned symbolic dynamics are then used by an explicit symbolic reasoner (e.g., a theorem prover or SAT solver) to perform counterfactual planning and causal analysis. The synergy creates a system that can answer 'what-if' questions and formulate plans based on discovered first principles, without ever being exposed to a task reward, leading to robust and generalizable reasoning that is verifiable by design.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmacokinetic Drug Delivery Models for Exploration Noise Scheduling in DDPG
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from pharmacology to the problem of exploration in Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) algorithms. In drug delivery, pharmacokinetic models are used to design sophisticated temporal dosing schedules (e.g., bolus+infusion, controlled-release) to optimize therapeutic effect. The analogous solution is to structure the exploration noise process in DDPG not as simple random noise or uniform decay, but as a carefully designed temporal schedule inspired by these drug delivery profiles. The adapted idea is to systematically study the impact of these alternative, biologically-inspired exploration noise processes—such as schedules mimicking initial bolus injections, sustained infusions, or pulsed deliveries—on learning efficiency, stability, and final policy performance in continuous control tasks.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware Hybrid Planning with DDPG via Certainty-Bounded Rollouts
ideas.description: This system integrates DDPG with a probabilistic dynamics model that estimates its own epistemic uncertainty. A planner generates on-policy trajectories for the DDPG actor, but only from states and within action ranges where model uncertainty is below a calibrated threshold. This creates a hybrid policy: in well-explored, certain regions of the state space, the agent benefits from efficient, planned exploration and policy refinement; in novel or uncertain regions, it falls back to robust, model-free DDPG updates. The uncertainty threshold is adapted online to balance the sample efficiency of planning with the safety of model-free learning.

ideas.title: Ensemble Forecasting for Robust Decision-Making
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core solution from Numerical Weather Prediction—ensemble forecasting—to the problem of scaling parallel imagination in AI. In weather science, running an ensemble of diverse, parallel simulations from perturbed initial conditions provides a robust, probabilistic forecast superior to any single run. Adapted here, an AI agent would run thousands of diverse imagination rollouts in parallel, each initialized with deliberate variations (e.g., in latent state, noise, or sub-model parameters). The aggregation mechanism is sophisticated: rather than just averaging, it analyzes the distribution of outcomes to construct a probabilistic 'decision forecast.' This identifies actions leading to high-probability desirable states, surfaces critical moments where small changes lead to vastly different futures, and selects decisions that are robust (low outcome variance) across the massive ensemble, thereby scaling imagination into a reliable tool for complex, uncertain environments.

ideas.title: Stabilized Asynchronous DDPG with Policy Consensus and Divergence Filtering
ideas.description: This research investigates a robust asynchronous framework for DDPG that directly mitigates the policy divergence inherent in naive parallelization. The core innovation is a Policy Consensus Buffer that collects proposed gradient updates from parallel actors and applies a consistency-checking mechanism before updating the central networks, ensuring updates reinforce a coherent policy direction. This is combined with a per-actor experience replay that is periodically synchronized with a filtered subset of high-consensus transitions from other actors, maintaining sample diversity while preserving training stability. The system aims to achieve near-linear scaling in data collection while maintaining, or even improving, the sample efficiency and final performance of standard DDPG.

ideas.title: Generative Imagination via Adversarial Counterfactual Simulation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ensemble forecasting method from climate science to the problem of AI imagination. In weather prediction, ensembles are created by running a physical model from multiple perturbed initial conditions to explore a range of plausible futures. Analogously, we propose using a generative adversarial network (GAN) or variational autoencoder (VAE) to synthesize a diverse set of plausible, slightly altered representations of a current state (perturbed 'initial conditions'). This 'imagination ensemble' is then fed into a learned dynamics model to simulate multiple coherent future trajectories. This approach moves beyond generating a single imagined future to producing a distribution of counterfactual scenarios, enabling more robust planning under uncertainty by quantifying the variety and likelihood of possible outcomes.

ideas.description: This research direction proposes running thousands of parallel imagination rollouts, but each rollout uses a drastically simplified, component-limited variant of the I2A architecture. By aggregating results across this massive, diverse population of minimalist 'imaginations', the system can statistically identify which architectural components are essential for planning benefit on a given task and which are superfluous. This creates a powerful experimental framework for reverse-engineering the principles of effective model-based reasoning, moving from empirical scaling laws to fundamental understanding.

ideas.title: Immunological Adversarial Training for Imagination Models
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea ('Assume model is adversarial or deceptive') via analogy to immunology. In vaccinology, the immune system is made robust by controlled exposure to weakened pathogens, teaching it to recognize and neutralize future threats. The analogous solution is adapted by proposing 'Immunological Adversarial Training' for imagination models. The research would involve designing a training regimen where the model is systematically exposed to its own generated outputs that are engineered to be biased, misleading, or deceptive—acting as 'adversarial antigens'. The objective is to force the model to develop internal validation or consistency-checking mechanisms, analogous to immunological memory, thereby increasing its robustness when its own imaginative processes become the source of adversarial bias, rather than just external inputs.

ideas.title: Bootstrapped Neuro-Symbolic Interface for Open-World Planning
ideas.description: This system learns an interpretable, formal representation language directly from interaction, eliminating the need for a pre-defined symbolic ontology. A neural perception module is trained to propose candidate logical predicates from raw observations, while a symbolic reasoner operates on these predicates to construct and verify plans. Through iterative self-supervision and constraint satisfaction, the two components co-evolve, resulting in a symbolic reasoning framework that is both verifiable and scalable to new domains without manual engineering.

ideas.title: Cognitively Frugal Imagination with Dynamic Relevance Pruning
ideas.description: This system runs massive parallel imagination but avoids decision paralysis by implementing a fast, learned relevance estimator that continuously scores and filters rollouts. It dynamically forms clusters of semantically similar futures and allocates 'cognitive budget' only to the most promising or informative branches. The final decision is made by aggregating insights from this curated, context-aware set of futures, ensuring robustness without combinatorial overload. This creates a scalable imagination core that is both expansive and strategically focused.

ideas.title: A Unified Theory-Practice Framework for Exploration Noise in Continuous Control
ideas.description: This research develops a principled framework to select and design exploration noise for DDPG by linking the statistical properties of noise processes to the structural characteristics of environments. It moves beyond empirical comparison by first establishing theoretical hypotheses—for instance, correlating noise autocorrelation with the temporal smoothness of optimal actions. These hypotheses are then rigorously tested on a curated set of diagnostic environments before the derived principles are applied to optimize noise selection for complex benchmarks like MuJoCo, ensuring results are both explainable and generalizable.

ideas.title: Cross-Audited Adversarial Imagination: Detecting and Analyzing Deception in Model-Based Rollouts
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to detect and analyze deceptive or systematically biased predictions from an imagination-based model. It employs a small ensemble of auditor models to establish a baseline of expected predictions, against which the primary model's rollouts are compared to flag potential deception. The system also includes a controlled adversarial training phase where the primary model is explicitly optimized to produce misleading yet internally consistent trajectories, creating known deception benchmarks. This allows for the systematic study of robustness by characterizing the divergence patterns between the primary model and the auditor consensus, enabling the development of detection metrics and mitigation strategies for deceptive model-based planning.

ideas.title: Synthesize Imagination Procedures via Chemical-Reaction-Inspired Rule Assembly
ideas.description: This idea adapts the modular synthesis paradigm from synthetic organic chemistry to the problem of generating interpretable, compositional imagination procedures via program induction. In chemistry, complex molecules are built systematically from a library of verified reaction mechanisms and molecular building blocks, ensuring predictability and interpretability of the final structure. Analogously, we propose to construct imagination procedures by synthesizing programs from a curated library of primitive, interpretable cognitive operations (e.g., 'extrapolate', 'mask-and-infill', 'conceptual blend') and a set of compositional grammar rules that define how these operations can be validly combined. A program synthesis engine, guided by this rule-based constraint system and an objective function (e.g., novelty, coherence, task utility), searches the space of valid combinations to generate novel imagination procedures. This approach guarantees the interpretability of the resulting procedures due to their compositional nature from known primitives, while the rule-based assembly, inspired by chemical reaction logic, ensures the generated 'cognitive recipes' are structurally sound and functionally reliable.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Temporal Chunking via Geological Stratigraphy Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts the geological solution of stratigraphic analysis to the problem of scaling imagination to extremely long horizons in sparse-reward tasks. In stratigraphy, geologists reconstruct Earth's multi-billion-year history from a sparse, fragmented rock record by identifying and correlating key temporal markers (like index fossils or magnetic reversals). We propose developing methods where an agent learns to identify analogous, sparse 'temporal marker' events from its own experience—rare successes, significant state changes, or novel observations. These markers are then used to construct a hierarchical, multi-scale encoding of the temporal sequence, creating a cognitive stratigraphy. This allows the agent to chunk time, imagine long-term consequences by interpolating between markers, and efficiently encode vast experience for planning, effectively scaling its imaginative horizon by orders of magnitude.

ideas.title: Counterfactual-Constrained Inverse Imagination for Robust Model Inference
ideas.description: This approach addresses the ill-posedness of the original inverse I2A problem by not seeking a single 'inferred imagination' but a constrained family of models. The system generates counterfactual policy suggestions—small deviations from the observed successful policy—and uses the real environment's response to these probes to rule out inconsistent dynamics models. The output is a robust characterization of the necessary and sufficient environmental beliefs that the successful policy must have held, along with a measure of uncertainty over alternative dynamics.

ideas.description: This idea proposes solving the challenge of applying DDPG to real-world robots by drawing an analogy from phased clinical trials in pharmaceutical development. The analogous solution involves a structured, multi-phase progression from controlled simulation to full deployment, with rigorous safety and performance checkpoints at each stage. The adapted research idea is to implement a 'Phase Transition' training regime for DDPG, where the robot policy graduates through distinct real-world testing phases (e.g., controlled lab, lab with noise, final environment) only after a meta-controller verifies stability and safety criteria, thereby systematically managing risk and sample inefficiency during physical training.
